item.title: Akira Kurosawa
item.description: Movement: Japanese Golden Age Cinema Akira Kurosawa made cinema that moves.

item.title: Alain Resnais
item.description: Movement: French New Wave Branch: Left Bank Alain Resnais came to features from documentary—most famously Night and Fog (1955), a confrontation with the Holocaust that remains one of cinema’s most devastating uses of archival footage.

item.title: Alfred Hitchcock
item.description: Movement: Classical Hollywood cinema Alfred Hitchcock worked inside the classical system more completely than any director in this vault so far and did more to undermine it from within.

item.title: Andrei Tarkovsky
item.description: Movement: Soviet Poetic Cinema Andrei Tarkovsky made seven features in twenty-five years, and each one feels like it took a lifetime to create.

item.title: Billy Wilder
item.description: Movement: Classical Hollywood cinema Billy Wilder worked inside the classical system as completely as Hitchcock — studio productions, star vehicles, genre contracts — and used it to say things the system was designed to prevent.

item.title: Chantal Akerman
item.description: Movement: Modernist cinema Chantal Akerman saw Godard’s Pierrot le fou at fifteen in Brussels and decided to make films.

item.title: Chris Marker
item.description: Movement: French New Wave Branch: Left Bank Chris Marker may be the most elusive major filmmaker of the twentieth century.

item.title: David Lynch
item.description: Movement: American Postmodern Cinema David Lynch makes films that operate like the unconscious.

item.title: Dino Risi
item.description: Movement: Italian Post-Neorealism (adjacent) Mode: Commedia all’italiana Dino Risi made comedies that refused to stay comedies.

item.title: Federico Fellini
item.description: Movement: Italian Post-Neorealism Federico Fellini started as a neorealist screenwriter and ended as cinema’s great dreamer.
